summary: Gruber argues that news organizations charging for online content is a fundamentally flawed strategy. He contends that readers perceive physical newspapers as worth paying for because they're tangible objects, but don't feel the same about web pages. Pay walls also prevent linking, which is how sites gain traffic, and paid sites must compete against free online-only publications. The real path forward is lean organizations where nearly everyone produces content, funded by advertising rather than reader fees. He points to his own site and Josh Marshall's TPM Media as models that work. The executives pushing pay walls are the very people whose roles would be eliminated in a viable digital news operation.
key_points: Pay walls are flawed because readers perceive physical papers as worth paying for but not web pages, and walls prevent the linking that drives traffic
The viable model for digital publishing is charging advertisers, not readers — Daring Fireball isn't 'free,' it just charges sponsors instead of readers
Sustainable digital news organizations are lean operations where nearly every employee produces editorial content, unlike legacy newspapers with massive management bureaucracies
Legacy news companies are asking the wrong question — 'How do we support our existing structure?' instead of 'How do we keep publishing good content?'
The executives making these decisions are the ones whose jobs would be cut in a viable restructuring, creating an inherent conflict of interest
money_quotes: When you pay a dollar for a newspaper it feels like you're paying for the actual stack of paper, and it feels like a fair price. That just isn't the case with web pages.
I've argued that Daring Fireball is not "free", it's just that I don't charge readers, I charge advertisers and sponsors.
What works in today's web landscape are lean and mean organizations with little or no management bureaucracy — operations where nearly every employee is working on producing actual content.

The question these companies should be asking is, "How do we keep reporting and publishing good content?" Instead, though, they're asking "How do we keep making enough money to support our existing management and advertising divisions?"
The decision-makers — the executives sitting atop large non-editorial management bureaucracies — are exactly the people who need to go if newspapers are going to remain profitable.
It is difficult to get a man to understand something when his salary depends upon his not understanding it.
key_insight: Legacy news organizations can't adapt to digital because the executives deciding strategy are the very bureaucratic overhead that needs to be eliminated for the business to survive.
